explanation_md: The vector starts as {false, false, false, false}. Setting v[1] and v[3] to true gives {false, true, false, true}. Printing the bool values (0 for false, 1 for true) without separators produces `0101`.

prompt_md:
Why does `for (bool& bit : bits)` fail to compile when `bits` is a `std::vector<bool>`?

a) Range-based for loops do not support `bool` element types
b) `std::vector<bool>` subscript returns a proxy object, not a real `bool&`
c) The `&` syntax is not allowed in range-based for loop variable declarations
d) `std::vector<bool>` stores chars internally, so the type mismatch causes the error

options.a: Range-based for loops do not support `bool` element types
options.b: `std::vector<bool>` subscript returns a proxy object, not a real `bool&`
options.c: The `&` syntax is not allowed in range-based for loop variable declarations
options.d: `std::vector<bool>` stores chars internally, so the type mismatch causes the error

explanation_md: The C++ standard allows `std::vector<bool>` to bit-pack elements. Because individual bits have no addresses, the subscript operator returns a proxy instead of a real `bool&`. The compiler cannot bind a `bool&` to a proxy, so `bool& bit` is a compile error. `auto bit` works because it deduces the proxy type.

prompt_md:
You have `std::vector<bool> flags;` and need to pass `flags[2]` to a function `void enable(bool& b) { b = true; }`. What is the cleanest fix?

a) Change the function to `void enable(std::vector<bool>::reference b) { b = true; }`
b) Copy the value out, call the function on the copy, then write it back
c) Use `flags.at(2)` instead of `flags[2]` to get a real reference
d) Cast `flags[2]` to `bool&` with `reinterpret_cast`

options.a: Change the function to `void enable(std::vector<bool>::reference b) { b = true; }`
options.b: Copy the value out, call the function on the copy, then write it back
options.c: Use `flags.at(2)` instead of `flags[2]` to get a real reference

explanation_md: Option (a) is the canonical solution: `std::vector<bool>::reference` is the proxy type that `operator[]` and `at()` return, and it supports assignment. Option (b) works but does not actually call through `enable` — it sidesteps the issue rather than fixing it. Option (c) is wrong: `at(2)` returns the same proxy as `operator[]`. Option (d) is undefined behaviour.

options.a: `bool& b` cannot bind to the proxy returned by iterating a `std::vector<bool>` — use `auto b` or `bool b`
options.b: The function parameter should be `std::vector<bool>&` not `const std::vector<bool>&`
options.c: Range-based for loops do not work with `const` vector parameters
options.d: The count variable should be `std::size_t` not `int`

explanation_md: Iterating a `std::vector<bool>` yields proxy objects, not real `bool` values. Binding a `bool&` to a proxy is a compile error. The fix is `auto b` (deduces proxy type) or `bool b` (copies the value into a plain bool). Options (b), (c), and (d) are incorrect diagnoses.
